topline: The US Supreme Court hands Donald Trump sweeping new powers as global tensions simmer with Pakistan striking Afghanistan, US-Iran nuclear talks stalling, and a deadly funeral shooting rocks Germany.

stories.headline: US Supreme Court 'Supercharges' Donald Trump's Powers In Major Ruling
stories.what: The United States Supreme Court has handed down a landmark ruling that significantly expands the executive powers of President Donald Trump. Described as 'supercharging' his authority, the decision marks a consequential shift in the balance of power between the US presidency and other branches of government. The ruling has drawn immediate attention globally for its potential to reshape how Trump governs for the remainder of his term.
stories.lens: A stronger US presidency directly affects India's bilateral ties, trade negotiations, and the treatment of the Indian diaspora in America — making this ruling matter well beyond Washington.
stories.article:
The United States Supreme Court has issued a sweeping ruling that dramatically amplifies the executive powers of President Donald Trump, in what observers are calling one of the most consequential decisions in recent American constitutional history. The ruling, described widely as 'supercharging' Trump's authority, tilts the institutional balance firmly toward the presidency.

The decision is expected to give Trump greater latitude to act unilaterally across a range of policy areas — from immigration and trade to foreign policy — with reduced checks from Congress or the courts. Legal analysts say the ruling could set a precedent that outlasts the current administration, reshaping the office of the presidency for years to come.

For India, the implications are significant. The US remains India's largest trading partner and a key strategic ally, and a more empowered Trump presidency could accelerate or complicate ongoing negotiations on tariffs, technology transfers, and defence cooperation. The Indian diaspora in the United States, one of the largest and most influential in the country, may also feel the downstream effects of executive actions enabled by the ruling.

The decision will likely face intense political scrutiny and legal debate in the coming weeks, as opponents warn of an erosion of democratic checks and balances. How Trump chooses to wield these expanded powers will be watched closely in New Delhi and capitals around the world.
key_facts: US Supreme Court ruling significantly expands President Trump's executive powers
Decision described as 'supercharging' presidential authority
Ruling has major implications for US foreign and domestic policy

stories.headline: India To End War-Time Petrol And Diesel Sale Restrictions From July 1
stories.what: India will lift restrictions on the sale of petrol and diesel from July 1, easing curbs that were put in place as war-time measures following the military tensions with Pakistan. The move signals a return to normalcy in the fuel retail sector after a period of heightened national security protocols. The lifting of restrictions is expected to ease the supply chain for fuel distributors and consumers across the country.
stories.lens: For millions of Indians who drive, run businesses, or depend on logistics, the end of fuel sale restrictions from July 1 means easier access to petrol and diesel — a direct relief after weeks of wartime curbs.
stories.article:
India will end restrictions on the sale of petrol and diesel from July 1, the government has confirmed, lifting war-time curbs that had been imposed in the wake of the military standoff with Pakistan. The move marks one of the most visible signs of a return to normalcy on the home front following a period of heightened security measures that touched everyday life across the country.

The restrictions had placed limits on fuel sales as part of a broader package of emergency measures adopted during the conflict period. Petrol pumps in several regions had operated under constraints, affecting consumers, transport operators, and industrial users. The July 1 deadline gives distributors and retailers a clear date to resume unrestricted operations.

The decision reflects the government's assessment that the immediate threat posture has eased sufficiently to allow normal commercial activity to resume in the energy sector. It is also likely to provide some relief to the economy, as fuel availability is a critical input for agriculture, manufacturing, and logistics.

For ordinary Indians, the end of restrictions translates to fewer queues, greater certainty of supply, and the removal of an inconvenience that had become a daily reminder of the conflict. The fuel sector will be watching closely to ensure that the transition on July 1 is smooth and that supply chains are fully restored from day one.
key_facts: Petrol and diesel sale restrictions to be lifted from July 1
Curbs were imposed as war-time measures during India-Pakistan tensions
Lifting restrictions signals return to normalcy in fuel retail

stories.headline: Indian Man Venkatesh Doppalaud From Andhra Pradesh Found Dead In Kansas Floods
stories.what: Venkatesh Doppalaud, an Indian man from Andhra Pradesh, has been found dead in Kansas, USA, after his car was swept away by floodwaters. Locals reportedly witnessed the car being carried into the water. An MLA from Andhra Pradesh had earlier sought help tracing a youth from the state who went missing in the US floods, believed to be the same individual. The tragedy adds to a growing toll of Indians caught in severe weather events abroad.
stories.lens: With a large Telugu-speaking diaspora in the United States, the death of Venkatesh Doppalaud will resonate deeply across Andhra Pradesh and the broader Indian-American community.
stories.article:
Venkatesh Doppalaud, an Indian national from Andhra Pradesh, has been found dead in the state of Kansas after his car was swept away by raging floodwaters, according to multiple Indian news reports. Witnesses at the scene reportedly saw the vehicle being carried into the water, leaving little chance of survival in the severe flooding that has struck parts of the United States in recent days.

The tragedy had attracted political attention in Andhra Pradesh even before his body was recovered, with a local MLA seeking help from authorities to trace the missing youth. His death has now been confirmed, bringing grief to his family and the wider Telugu community both in India and in the United States, which has one of the largest concentrations of Telugu-speaking immigrants anywhere in the world.

The incident comes amid broader flooding events across parts of the US that have claimed multiple lives. For Indian families who send children abroad for education or employment — particularly to states like Kansas with significant Indian student and professional populations — the news serves as a stark reminder of the dangers of extreme weather.

The Andhra Pradesh government and diaspora organisations are expected to extend support to the bereaved family. Consular officials from India are likely to be involved in coordinating the repatriation of the deceased.
key_facts: Venkatesh Doppalaud from Andhra Pradesh found dead in Kansas, US
His car was swept away by floodwaters; locals witnessed it
Andhra Pradesh MLA had sought help tracing the missing youth

stories.headline: US-Iran Nuclear Talks In Doubt As Tehran Says No Technical Meeting Expected Soon
stories.what: Diplomatic uncertainty clouds the US-Iran nuclear negotiations, with Iran saying no technical meeting with Washington is expected in the coming days, even as President Trump claimed the two sides would hold fresh talks in Qatar. The contradictory signals come after weekend clashes between the two countries, and Trump separately suggested Iran had requested the meeting — a claim Iranian officials denied. The confusion underscores deep mistrust on both sides ahead of the Doha talks.
stories.lens: India imports significant quantities of oil from the region and maintains critical economic ties with both Iran and the US, making any escalation or breakthrough in this standoff directly consequential for Indian energy prices and diplomacy.
stories.article:
The diplomatic path between the United States and Iran grew murkier on Sunday, with Tehran stating publicly that no technical meeting with Washington was expected in the coming days — directly contradicting claims by President Donald Trump that fresh talks were scheduled in Qatar. The conflicting statements, following a weekend of military clashes between the two sides, have cast serious doubt over the prospects for near-term progress on Iran's nuclear programme.

Trump told reporters that Iran had requested a meeting in Doha, but Iranian officials flatly denied making any such request and said no meeting had been scheduled. The disconnect reflects the deep mistrust that has characterised the on-again, off-again diplomatic process, complicated now by renewed hostilities. Earlier, there had been reports that the US and Iran had agreed to halt attacks ahead of the Doha talks.

The Strait of Hormuz, through which a significant share of the world's oil passes, remains a pressure point in the standoff. A separate report noted Iran's rivalry with Oman over who holds decisive influence over the strategically vital waterway, adding a further layer of regional complexity to an already tense situation.

For India, the stakes are considerable. New Delhi has historically maintained carefully calibrated ties with Tehran while also deepening its strategic partnership with Washington. A renewed military confrontation in the Persian Gulf could disrupt energy supplies and force difficult choices on Indian policymakers who have long sought to balance both relationships.
key_facts: Iran says no technical meeting with US expected in coming days
Trump claimed Iran requested fresh talks in Qatar; Iran denied this
Weekend clashes preceded the diplomatic confusion
Earlier reports had suggested a mutual agreement to halt attacks

stories.headline: 5 Dead In Shooting At Funeral In Germany, Arrests Made
stories.what: A shooting in Germany left five people dead, with arrests made following the incident. The attack, reported by multiple Indian news outlets, is among the most serious gun violence incidents in Germany in recent memory. Germany has some of Europe's strictest gun laws, making a mass shooting of this scale particularly shocking. Investigations are underway to determine the motive and whether further suspects are at large.
stories.lens: Mass shootings in Western democracies with strict gun laws serve as a reminder of the global challenge of preventing extremist violence — a subject with direct resonance for Indian security policymakers.
stories.article:
A shooting in Germany left five people dead on Sunday, with authorities making arrests in the immediate aftermath of the attack. The incident, reported by NDTV, has sent shockwaves through Germany and drawn international attention given the country's reputation for stringent gun control legislation.

Germany enforces some of the strictest firearms regulations in Europe, making a shooting that results in five fatalities an extraordinary and alarming event. Full details of the location, circumstances, and the identities of victims and perpetrators are still emerging, but authorities moved quickly to detain suspects.

The attack comes at a time of heightened concern across Europe about public safety, with governments reassessing security protocols at public gatherings. German authorities will face pressure to explain how a shooting of this magnitude occurred despite the country's robust legal framework around weapons.

The incident will be closely monitored in India, which has its own ongoing concerns about radicalisation and public safety. Germany is also home to a significant Indian and South Asian diaspora, and the Indian embassy is expected to monitor the situation to ensure the community's safety in the aftermath.
key_facts: 5 people killed in a shooting in Germany
Arrests were made following the incident
Germany has some of Europe's strictest gun control laws

stories.headline: India and Maldives open week-long FTA negotiations, aiming to reset strained economic ties
stories.what: India and the Maldives have begun a week-long round of formal negotiations for a free trade agreement, in a significant diplomatic and economic development for bilateral relations. The talks mark a substantive effort to place trade at the heart of a relationship that has experienced political turbulence in recent years. A successful FTA could deepen supply and investment links between the two Indian Ocean neighbours and reinforce India's strategic position in the region.
stories.lens: An FTA with the Maldives matters beyond trade volumes — it is India's tool to rebuild economic influence in a neighbouring island nation that has been courted heavily by China.
stories.article:
India and the Maldives have formally launched a week-long round of free trade agreement negotiations, marking a new chapter in bilateral relations between the two Indian Ocean neighbours, Firstpost reported.

The talks come after a period of diplomatic tension, during which the Maldives under President Mohamed Muizzu had initially leaned toward distancing itself from New Delhi, before pragmatic economic realities drew both sides back to the negotiating table. The FTA negotiations signal a deliberate decision by both governments to anchor the relationship in concrete economic interests rather than allowing it to be defined by geopolitical posturing.

For India, an FTA with the Maldives would secure preferential access for Indian goods and services in a market that relies heavily on imports for virtually everything it consumes — from food and medicine to construction material and fuel. India is already among the Maldives' largest trading partners and a principal source of tourists visiting the archipelago.

The strategic dimension is equally significant. The Maldives sits astride crucial Indian Ocean sea lanes, and India has long sought to maintain close ties to ensure that Chinese infrastructure and military presence in the archipelago does not expand unchecked. Locking in an FTA would deepen economic interdependence and give New Delhi a durable structural lever in the relationship. The week-long negotiation schedule suggests both sides are targeting a relatively swift conclusion.
key_facts: India-Maldives FTA negotiations launched, scheduled for one week
Talks aim to deepen trade and investment ties between neighbours
Strategic context: Maldives sits on key Indian Ocean sea lanes
Signals diplomatic reset after period of bilateral tension

stories.headline: Apple accuses India of 'copy-pasting' rivals' claims in antitrust probe, escalating CCI standoff
stories.what: Apple has accused India's competition regulator of lifting allegations directly from the company's rivals and incorporating them wholesale into its antitrust investigation, according to a Reuters exclusive. The charge, described as 'copy-pasting' rivals' claims, represents a sharp escalation in Apple's pushback against the Competition Commission of India. The dispute centres on the CCI's investigation into Apple's App Store practices, which mirrors similar probes in the EU and other jurisdictions. Apple's accusation raises serious questions about the process and independence of the regulatory inquiry.
stories.lens: If Apple's 'copy-paste' allegation holds weight, it could force a reset of India's antitrust investigation — with significant implications for how India regulates Big Tech going forward.
stories.article:
Apple has formally accused India's competition regulator of copying allegations made by its rivals and inserting them into the official antitrust investigation against the company, Reuters reported exclusively, in what amounts to the most direct public challenge yet to the Competition Commission of India's probe.

The investigation by the CCI focuses on Apple's App Store policies — particularly its rules around in-app payments, commission rates, and restrictions on third-party app distribution — practices that have also drawn regulatory fire in Europe, the United States and South Korea. Apple's accusation is that the CCI did not independently develop its case but instead relied on submissions made by competing companies, effectively allowing rivals to shape a government regulatory action.

The allegation puts the CCI in an uncomfortable spotlight. Regulators worldwide have been scrutinising Apple's App Store as a potential abuse of dominance, but Apple's argument is that India's investigation lacks procedural integrity if its factual basis was drawn from adversarial third-party complaints without independent verification.

Apple has been investing heavily in India — ramping up iPhone manufacturing through Foxconn and Tata, and expanding its retail presence — making the regulatory environment here increasingly consequential for the company. The outcome of the CCI probe could force changes to how Apple operates its App Store in India, affecting millions of developers and consumers. How the regulator responds to Apple's procedural challenge will be closely watched by the broader tech industry.
key_facts: Apple accuses CCI of 'copy-pasting' rivals' claims into antitrust investigation
CCI probe focuses on Apple App Store practices and commissions
Allegation raises questions about procedural independence of India's antitrust inquiry
Reported exclusively by Reuters

stories.headline: Cabinet commits Rs 30,000 crore additional investment to NIIF to turbocharge infrastructure build-out
stories.what: The Union Cabinet has approved an additional investment commitment of Rs 30,000 crore into the National Investment and Infrastructure Fund (NIIF), a move aimed at accelerating infrastructure investment across India and attracting institutional capital into the country. The NIIF, set up as a sovereign wealth fund-like vehicle, channels public and private money into roads, ports, energy and logistics projects. The fresh capital infusion is one of the largest single top-ups to the fund and signals the government's intent to keep infrastructure spending at the centre of India's growth strategy.
stories.lens: A Rs 30,000 crore injection into NIIF means faster highways, ports and power projects — and more private institutional money flowing into the backbone of India's economy.
stories.article:
The Union Cabinet on Sunday approved an additional investment commitment of Rs 30,000 crore into the National Investment and Infrastructure Fund, the government-backed infrastructure investment platform that pools sovereign and institutional capital to fund large-scale projects across India.

The NIIF was established to bridge the gap between India's infrastructure financing needs and what the budget alone can deliver, drawing in pension funds, sovereign wealth funds and development finance institutions as co-investors. The fresh Rs 30,000 crore commitment is designed to act as a catalyst, with every rupee of government money expected to unlock multiple rupees of private and institutional capital.

The announcement comes as India accelerates its infrastructure push, with roads, railways, ports, airports, and clean energy pipelines all requiring sustained long-term financing. By topping up the NIIF, the government is signalling that it intends to use the fund as a key vehicle to crowd-in global and domestic institutional investors who need sovereign-backed structures before committing at scale.

The move is also expected to strengthen India's pitch to overseas pension funds and sovereign wealth funds looking for stable, long-duration infrastructure assets. Officials indicated the additional commitment would help the NIIF catalyse institutional capital into priority sectors and support India's broader National Infrastructure Pipeline targets.
key_facts: Cabinet approves Rs 30,000 crore additional commitment to NIIF
Funds aimed at accelerating infrastructure investment across India
NIIF designed to catalyse private and institutional capital alongside sovereign funds

stories.headline: India's pharmaceutical exports discover a powerful new growth engine in Brazil
stories.what: India's pharmaceutical export industry is finding Brazil emerging as a significant new growth market, offering a major diversification opportunity beyond traditional export destinations. Brazil's large population, growing generic medicines demand and regulatory openings are combining to make it an increasingly attractive destination for Indian drug makers. The development is strategically important as India seeks to reduce its dependence on the US market, which faces tariff uncertainty and pricing pressure.
stories.lens: Brazil as a new pharma growth engine matters because it reduces Indian drugmakers' over-reliance on the US market and signals that India's generic medicine story still has significant global runway.
stories.article:
India's pharmaceutical export sector is gaining meaningful traction in Brazil, with the South American giant emerging as a new growth engine for Indian generic and branded medicine exporters, Moneycontrol reported.

Brazil is one of the world's largest pharmaceutical markets, with a healthcare system that places a strong emphasis on generic substitution and a population of over 200 million that generates substantial demand for affordable medicines. Indian pharmaceutical companies, long dominant in supplying generics to regulated markets in the US and Europe, are increasingly positioning Brazil as a priority expansion market.

The timing is significant. Indian pharma exports to the United States have faced headwinds from pricing pressure, regulatory scrutiny by the US FDA, and the threat of import tariffs under shifting US trade policy. Brazil offers a large, less-contested market where Indian companies' cost competitiveness in generic manufacturing translates directly into commercial advantage.

For India's broader export strategy, the Brazil story illustrates a wider push to diversify pharmaceutical export destinations toward emerging and developing markets in Latin America, Africa and Southeast Asia. If the growth momentum in Brazil is sustained, it could materially shift the geographic revenue mix of India's USD 25-plus billion pharmaceutical export industry and provide a more resilient foundation for future growth.
key_facts: Brazil emerging as a new growth market for India's pharma exports
India seeks to diversify exports beyond the US market
Brazil has one of the world's largest generic medicine markets
Reported by Moneycontrol

stories.headline: Six financial rules change from July 1: ITR deadlines, Aadhaar updates, passport fees and bank norms all shift
stories.what: A clutch of six significant financial and regulatory changes will come into effect from July 1, touching everyday Indians across multiple domains — from income tax return filings and Aadhaar-related rules to passport fee revisions and updated banking norms. The changes affect both compliance timelines and the cost of routine services. Coming mid-year, they require citizens and businesses to quickly adapt to new requirements or face penalties and higher charges.
stories.lens: Six rule changes on a single date mean most working Indians will need to check their ITR status, Aadhaar records and bank accounts before July 1 or risk fines and higher costs.
stories.article:
July 1 brings a wave of financial and regulatory changes that will directly affect the daily lives and wallets of ordinary Indians, with six distinct rule shifts spanning income tax compliance, biometric identity, travel documents and banking operations coming into force simultaneously.

On the income tax front, the new rules touch ITR-related deadlines and procedures — taxpayers who have not yet filed or verified their returns need to act quickly to avoid penalties under the revised framework. Aadhaar-related changes are also among the six updates, affecting processes linked to the identity database that underpins everything from welfare delivery to banking KYC.

Passport fees are set to be revised from July 1, meaning Indians applying for new passports or renewals after that date will face a different fee schedule. Banking norms are also being updated, with rule changes that could affect account-holders' obligations, charges or service conditions depending on their bank and product type.

The simultaneous arrival of six changes makes July 1 an unusually busy compliance date. Financial advisers are urging individuals to review their pending ITR filings, check Aadhaar-linked records, and confirm their bank account details before the deadline to avoid unnecessary costs or procedural hurdles under the new regime.
key_facts: Six financial rule changes effective July 1
Covers ITR filing, Aadhaar, passport fees and bank norms
Taxpayers and account-holders urged to act before the deadline

stories.headline: Rs 91,500 crore and 2.45 lakh jobs at risk from India's digital rules, warns new report
stories.what: A new report has sounded the alarm on India's tightening digital regulations, warning that as much as Rs 91,500 crore in economic value and 2.45 lakh jobs could be under threat. The findings, reported by The Economic Times, suggest that overly restrictive digital rules risk undermining the country's startup engine. The scale of potential harm underscores how regulatory uncertainty has become a major concern for India's fast-growing digital economy.
stories.lens: For millions of Indians employed in or dependent on the startup ecosystem, regulatory overreach could directly shrink job opportunities and slow the digital economy that drives much of India's growth story.
stories.article:
India's startup ecosystem faces a serious economic threat from restrictive digital regulations, with a new report warning that Rs 91,500 crore in value and as many as 2.45 lakh jobs could be at stake if current policy trajectories are not reconsidered.

The report, highlighted by The Economic Times, paints a sobering picture of regulatory risk at a time when India has positioned itself as a global startup hub. According to the findings, overly stringent digital rules have the potential to slow startup formation and curtail venture capital funding — two pillars that have fuelled the country's tech boom over the past decade.

A companion survey reported by Mint adds nuance to the debate: Indian startups are less worried about artificial intelligence regulation than they are about data governance. The findings suggest that how India handles data — who owns it, how it is stored, and how it can be used — is the single biggest regulatory concern for founders and investors operating in the digital space.

Together, the reports signal a growing tension between the government's desire to regulate a maturing digital sector and the industry's need for a predictable, innovation-friendly environment. With global tech investment increasingly mobile, founders and VCs warn that heavy-handed rules could push capital and talent to more permissive jurisdictions.

The findings are likely to intensify lobbying by industry bodies ahead of any finalisation of India's digital regulatory framework, with stakeholders urging policymakers to strike a balance that protects consumers without stifling the entrepreneurial energy that has made India one of the world's top startup destinations.
key_facts: Rs 91,500 crore in economic value potentially at risk
2.45 lakh jobs could be threatened by restrictive digital rules
Data governance — not AI — is startups' top regulatory concern
Restrictive rules could slow startup formation and VC funding

stories.headline: India doesn't need its own ChatGPT to become an AI powerhouse, says NetApp CEO
stories.what: The chief executive of NetApp has argued that India does not need to build its own large language model akin to ChatGPT to emerge as a major force in artificial intelligence. Speaking to CNBC TV18, the NetApp CEO suggested that India's path to AI leadership lies in application, infrastructure, and talent rather than in developing foundational models from scratch. The remarks reflect a broader debate about where India should focus its AI ambitions and investments.
stories.lens: As India crafts its national AI strategy, this high-profile perspective from a global tech CEO shapes the conversation about where Indian companies and policymakers should actually direct their money and energy.
stories.article:
India does not need to build its own version of ChatGPT to become a genuine AI powerhouse — that is the view of the CEO of NetApp, expressed in an interview with CNBC TV18 that is likely to resonate across boardrooms and policy circles.

The argument challenges a strand of Indian tech nationalism that has called for homegrown foundational AI models to compete with American and Chinese counterparts. The NetApp chief's position is that India's competitive advantage lies elsewhere: in deploying AI across its vast economy, building the data infrastructure to support it, and leveraging the country's deep pool of engineering talent.

The perspective is not without precedent. Several technology strategists have noted that building frontier large language models requires billions of dollars in compute costs and years of research — resources that are concentrated in a handful of American and Chinese firms. India, the argument goes, can extract enormous value by being a world-class consumer and customiser of AI rather than a frontier model builder.

The debate has practical consequences for how India allocates its investments in the sector — whether public and private capital flows toward foundational model research or toward AI-enabled services, healthcare, agriculture, and governance applications where Indian companies already show strength.

With India's government actively developing an AI mission and private investment in the sector growing, the NetApp CEO's comments add a notable international voice to a domestic conversation that will shape the country's technology trajectory for years to come.
key_facts: NetApp CEO says India need not build its own ChatGPT
India's AI edge lies in application and talent, not foundational models
Remarks made to CNBC TV18

stories.headline: 'Down But Not Out': New book chronicles near-death survival stories of India's top entrepreneurs
stories.what: Authors Subrata Mitra and Pankaj Mishra have launched a new book titled Down But Not Out, which documents the near-death moments — the crises, failures, and pivots — behind some of India's most celebrated entrepreneurial success stories. Reported by Business Standard, the book aims to offer an unfiltered account of the setbacks that preceded breakthrough success in the Indian startup world. It arrives at a time of renewed scrutiny of startup valuations and sustainability, making its lessons particularly timely.
stories.lens: At a moment when Indian startup culture tends to celebrate unicorn milestones over honest failure, a book that centres the near-collapse stories of real entrepreneurs offers a more grounded and instructive picture of what building a business actually requires.
stories.article:
A new book that pulls back the curtain on the darkest chapters of India's entrepreneurial journey has been launched by Subrata Mitra and Pankaj Mishra. Titled Down But Not Out, the work chronicles the near-death experiences — the funding crises, product failures, co-founder breakdowns, and pivotal moments of doubt — that preceded the rise of some of India's best-known business names.

The book, reported by Business Standard, is positioned as a counter-narrative to the triumphalist storytelling that typically surrounds India's startup ecosystem. While headlines celebrate billion-dollar valuations and successful IPOs, Down But Not Out focuses on the moments when founders came closest to giving up — and what pulled them back.

Subrata Mitra, a prominent figure in India's venture capital landscape as a partner at Accel India, and Pankaj Mishra, a veteran technology journalist and co-founder of the media platform FactorDaily, bring complementary perspectives to the project. Mitra has had a front-row seat to the inner workings of startups in crisis, while Mishra has spent years documenting the human stories behind India's tech sector.

The timing of the launch is notable. India's startup ecosystem has faced a prolonged funding winter, a rash of high-profile governance controversies, and a more sober public conversation about what sustainable growth actually looks like. In that context, a book that treats failure as instructive rather than shameful carries particular resonance.

Down But Not Out is likely to find an audience not just among founders and investors, but among the broader community of young Indians drawn to entrepreneurship — and looking for an honest account of the road ahead.
key_facts: Book titled 'Down But Not Out' launched by Subrata Mitra and Pankaj Mishra
Chronicles near-death crisis moments behind India's entrepreneurial rise
Subrata Mitra is a partner at Accel India; Pankaj Mishra is co-founder of FactorDaily

stories.headline: Government launches TRL Compass Platform, signs MoU with Data Security Council of India
stories.what: The Office of the Principal Scientific Adviser to the Government of India has launched the TRL Compass Platform and signed a Memorandum of Understanding with the Data Security Council of India (DSCI). The TRL Compass is designed to help assess and navigate technology readiness levels, providing a structured framework for moving innovations from the laboratory to the marketplace. The MoU with DSCI signals a push to embed data security considerations into India's technology development pipeline from an early stage.
stories.lens: By formalising a link between technology readiness assessment and data security standards, this initiative could shape how Indian deep-tech and software innovations are validated and commercialised — with direct implications for startups and research institutions.
stories.article:
The Office of the Principal Scientific Adviser (PSA) to the Government of India has launched the TRL Compass Platform and signed a formal partnership agreement with the Data Security Council of India (DSCI), according to an announcement from the Press Information Bureau.

The TRL Compass Platform is built around the concept of Technology Readiness Levels — a globally recognised scale used to assess how mature a technology is, from early-stage research all the way through to full deployment. By providing Indian innovators, startups, and research institutions with a structured compass to gauge where their technology stands, the platform aims to help bridge the often difficult gap between laboratory breakthroughs and real-world commercial applications.

The MoU with DSCI — India's premier industry body focused on data protection, cybersecurity, and privacy — adds a data governance dimension to the initiative. The partnership is intended to ensure that as technologies are assessed and scaled, data security and privacy standards are built in from the ground up rather than addressed as an afterthought.

The launch comes at a moment of heightened focus on both technology commercialisation and data regulation in India. The government has been pushing to accelerate deep-tech innovation through various programmes, and the PSA's office has played a central role in coordinating research and development policy.

For India's innovation ecosystem — spanning academic institutions, government labs, and startups — the TRL Compass offers a potential common language for measuring and communicating technological progress, while the DSCI partnership signals that security will be a foundational consideration in that journey.
key_facts: Office of Principal Scientific Adviser launches TRL Compass Platform
MoU signed with Data Security Council of India (DSCI)
Platform assesses Technology Readiness Levels for Indian innovations

stories.headline: Data governance, not AI, is Indian startups' biggest regulatory fear, survey finds
stories.what: A new survey reported by Mint has found that artificial intelligence regulation is not the primary concern keeping Indian startup founders up at night — data governance is. As India finalises its data protection framework, uncertainty around how data can be collected, stored, and used is creating more anxiety in the startup community than any AI-specific rules. The finding highlights a critical fault line in the relationship between India's booming digital sector and its evolving regulatory environment.
stories.lens: How India regulates data will directly determine whether startups can build competitive products — making this survey a useful guide for where founders, investors, and policymakers most urgently need clarity.
stories.article:
Indian startups are more worried about data governance than artificial intelligence regulation, according to a survey reported by Mint — a finding that cuts against the assumption that AI rules are the defining regulatory concern of the moment.

The survey captures the mood across India's startup ecosystem at a pivotal time, as the government moves toward implementing its data protection legislation. For founders building products that depend on user data — from fintech and healthtech to edtech and e-commerce — the rules around data localisation, consent, and cross-border transfers carry immediate, practical consequences for their business models.

By contrast, while AI regulation is a live global conversation, Indian startups appear to regard it as a more distant or manageable concern. This could reflect the fact that most Indian startups are applying AI rather than building foundational models, making them less exposed to the kind of AI-specific liability questions exercising policymakers in Europe and the United States.

The survey results arrive alongside a separate report warning that restrictive digital rules broadly could put Rs 91,500 crore in value and 2.45 lakh jobs at risk — underscoring that regulatory design choices in Delhi have billion-dollar stakes for the ecosystem.

For policymakers, the message from the startup community is clear: get data governance right first. The rules around data will shape India's digital economy more profoundly in the near term than any specific AI legislation, and founders are watching the legislative process closely.
key_facts: Data governance ranked as top regulatory concern by Indian startups
AI regulation considered less pressing than data rules
Survey reported by Mint

stories.headline: Pune Court Extends Custody of Siya Goyal and Chetan Till July 3 in Ketan Agarwal Murder Case
stories.what: A Pune court has extended the police custody of Siya Goyal and her alleged lover Chetan till July 3 in the murder case of Ketan Agarwal. Investigations have revealed a premeditated plan involving a switch from car to scooter, a codeword used as a signal, secret calls, and deleted chats. Police are now seeking to recover a burnt passport as part of their probe, and are also planning a 'gait analysis' of the accused.
stories.lens: The Ketan Agarwal murder case has gripped the country with its trail of calculated evidence — a burnt passport, deleted chats, and a codeword — illustrating how modern investigations now piece together digital and physical clues in premeditated crimes.
stories.article:
A Pune court extended the police custody of Siya Goyal and her alleged lover Chetan till July 3, as investigators continue to unravel what police describe as a carefully planned murder of Ketan Agarwal — a case that has become one of the most closely watched criminal investigations in India this year.

Police have disclosed a series of findings that point to deliberate preparation: the accused allegedly switched from a car to a scooter to avoid detection, used a codeword as a signal — reportedly Siya's act of sitting down was meant to indicate her own safety while triggering the attack — and communicated via secret calls. Investigators have also found deleted chats on the accused's devices, which forensic teams are attempting to recover.

Among the key reasons cited for the custody extension is the need to find a burnt passport, which police believe could be crucial to establishing the full scope of the conspiracy. Investigators are also planning a 'gait analysis' of the accused to match movements captured on surveillance footage at the scene.

The case has gripped public attention for its apparent mix of intimate betrayal and cold calculation. With custody extended and multiple forensic threads being pursued simultaneously, the investigation appears to be entering a more detailed evidential phase ahead of a formal chargesheet.
key_facts: Custody of Siya Goyal and Chetan extended till July 3
Police seeking to recover a burnt passport
Evidence includes secret calls, deleted chats, and a codeword signal
Gait analysis of accused being planned by police

stories.headline: West Bengal Government to Introduce Uniform Civil Code Bill; BJP Announces High-Powered Panel, Bill Likely in August Assembly Session
stories.what: The West Bengal government is set to introduce a Uniform Civil Code Bill in the state assembly, with BJP leader Suvendu Adhikari announcing a high-powered panel to draft the legislation. The Bill is likely to be tabled during the August assembly session, making West Bengal one of the few states to move formally on UCC after Uttarakhand. The development marks a significant escalation of the UCC debate in a state with a large Muslim minority population.
stories.lens: The UCC has been one of India's most politically charged legislative proposals — its introduction in West Bengal, a state with significant religious and cultural diversity, would be one of the most consequential state-level political moves of the year.
stories.article:
West Bengal is set to become one of the first states after Uttarakhand to formally move on a Uniform Civil Code, with the BJP government announcing plans to introduce a UCC Bill in the state assembly. Leader of Opposition Suvendu Adhikari announced the formation of a high-powered panel to prepare the legislation, with the Bill expected to be tabled in the August session of the assembly.

The UCC aims to replace personal laws — which currently govern marriage, divorce, inheritance, and adoption differently across religious communities — with a single set of civil rules applicable to all citizens regardless of faith. While the BJP has championed the UCC as a national priority, its implementation at the state level in West Bengal would carry exceptional political weight given the state's large Muslim minority and its history of communal sensitivity.

Uttarakhand became the first state to enact a UCC law in 2024, and several other BJP-governed states have expressed intent to follow. West Bengal, where the BJP is in power following the 2021 assembly elections, would be a far more politically complex arena for the legislation — one that is likely to generate intense debate both inside the assembly and across civil society.

Opposition parties and Muslim community organisations are expected to challenge the move vigorously. Legal experts noted that while states can legislate on many personal law matters, the constitutional and political dimensions of a state-level UCC will face significant scrutiny.
key_facts: West Bengal BJP government to introduce UCC Bill
Suvendu Adhikari announces high-powered drafting panel
Bill expected in August assembly session
Uttarakhand was the first state to enact a UCC law

stories.headline: Kashmir SIA Files 737-Page Chargesheet Against Yasin Malik in 1990 Sarla Bhat Murder Case — 36 Years On
stories.what: Kashmir's Special Investigation Agency has filed a 737-page chargesheet naming JKLF chief Yasin Malik as the mastermind in the 1990 rape and murder of Kashmiri Pandit nurse Sarla Bhat — a case that remained unsolved for 36 years. Malik and four other JKLF operatives have been named as accused. Bhat, who had been branded an 'informant', was raped, killed, and her murder became one of the landmark cases of the Kashmir terror era.
stories.lens: One of the most brutal, unresolved crimes of the Kashmir exodus — the rape and murder of a Kashmiri Pandit nurse — has finally reached court, with Yasin Malik, already serving a life sentence, now facing fresh charges.
stories.article:
Kashmir's Special Investigation Agency has filed a 737-page chargesheet in the 1990 rape and murder of Kashmiri Pandit nurse Sarla Bhat, naming JKLF chief Yasin Malik as the key accused and mastermind — bringing a case that haunted the community for 36 years to a formal courtroom reckoning.

Bhat was a nurse who was abducted, raped, and killed in 1990 at the height of the armed insurgency in Kashmir, during which hundreds of Kashmiri Pandits were targeted, killed, or forced to flee the Valley. She had reportedly been branded an 'informant' by militants — a label used to justify targeted killings during the period. Her murder became one of the most cited cases in accounts of the terror and ethnic cleansing faced by the Kashmiri Pandit community.

Along with Malik, four other JKLF operatives have been named in the chargesheet. Yasin Malik is already imprisoned, having been sentenced to life in a separate terror-funding case by a Delhi court in 2022. The SIA, which was set up specifically to reinvestigate cold cases from the insurgency era, has been building the case using forensic evidence, witness testimonies, and documentary records accumulated over decades.

The filing marks a significant moment for Kashmiri Pandit survivors and families who have long demanded justice for the violence of 1990. Civil society groups and political observers noted that bringing a chargesheet of this scope — 737 pages, naming a high-profile accused — signals the government's intent to pursue accountability for insurgency-era crimes that had gone largely unprosecuted.
key_facts: 737-page chargesheet filed by Kashmir SIA
Yasin Malik named as mastermind; 4 JKLF operatives also named
Sarla Bhat, a Kashmiri Pandit nurse, was raped and killed in 1990
Case unsolved for 36 years before chargesheet filing
Malik already serving life sentence in a terror-funding case

stories.headline: Delhi Cabinet Clears New EV Policy With ₹15,000-Crore Budget: Only Electric Autos From 2027, No Petrol Two-Wheelers From 2028
stories.what: The Delhi Cabinet has approved a sweeping new electric vehicle policy backed by a ₹15,000-crore budget, mandating that only electric auto-rickshaws be registered from 2027 and phasing out petrol two-wheelers by 2028. The policy includes zero road tax for electric cars and full tax relief, while hybrid vehicles will receive no subsidies. The move is one of the most aggressive EV transition timelines adopted by any Indian state.
stories.lens: For the millions of Delhi residents who own two-wheelers or rely on auto-rickshaws daily, this policy sets hard deadlines that will directly reshape how they commute and what vehicles they can buy in the coming years.
stories.article:
The Delhi Cabinet has approved a new electric vehicle policy with a ₹15,000-crore budget, setting firm timelines that will fundamentally change the capital's transport landscape — only electric auto-rickshaws from 2027 and a complete end to new petrol two-wheelers from 2028.

The policy also grants full road tax exemption to electric cars, making EV ownership significantly cheaper upfront in the capital. In a pointed departure from national trends, hybrid vehicles have been explicitly excluded from subsidies — signalling that Delhi's push is for pure electric technology, not transitional solutions. The government has framed the policy as essential to combat the city's chronic air pollution crisis, which ranks among the worst of any major world city.

Auto-rickshaws are a lifeline for last-mile connectivity across Delhi, and the 2027 deadline for new electric-only registrations will pressure the city's large fleet operators and individual drivers to plan transitions. Two-wheeler owners — who make up a substantial share of vehicle registrations — face a harder shift, with no new petrol bikes or scooters permitted from 2028 onwards.

The ₹15,000-crore budget signals the scale of state investment envisioned to support charging infrastructure, subsidies for buyers, and the retooling of the auto sector. Policy analysts noted that Delhi's timelines are among the most aggressive set by any Indian city and will be closely watched as a test case for urban EV transitions across the country.
key_facts: Delhi EV policy approved with ₹15,000-crore budget
Only electric auto-rickshaws permitted from 2027
Petrol two-wheelers phased out from 2028
Zero road tax for electric cars
No subsidies for hybrid vehicles

stories.headline: CBSE Relaxes Three-Language Policy for Classes 7-9, Exempts Current Class 10 Batch, Allows Two Foreign Language Options
stories.what: CBSE has relaxed its three-language policy for students in Classes 7, 8, and 9, and has exempted the current Class 10 batch from the requirement entirely. The board is also allowing two foreign language options, offering more flexibility to students across the country. The move comes amid pushback from states including Karnataka, where the state school body argued that Kannada cannot be treated as a third language under the CBSE framework.
stories.lens: For lakhs of students and parents navigating CBSE board requirements, this policy shift directly affects language choices at a critical stage of schooling — particularly in non-Hindi states where the three-language formula has been a source of tension.
stories.article:
CBSE has relaxed its three-language policy for students in Classes 7, 8, and 9, exempting the current Class 10 batch entirely from the requirement and opening up two foreign language options for students — a significant rollback amid resistance from multiple states.

The three-language formula, rooted in the National Education Policy, mandates that students learn three languages, including a regional language. Its implementation by CBSE had triggered sharp opposition from states like Karnataka, where the state school body publicly stated that Kannada could not be treated as a 'third language' — reflecting broader anxieties in southern states about linguistic imposition.

By exempting the current Class 10 batch, CBSE has offered immediate relief to students who were already mid-course and had not planned their language combination around the newer requirements. The addition of two foreign language options broadens the practical utility of the policy for students in urban schools where languages like French, German, or Japanese are offered.

Education policy observers noted that the relaxation reflects a pragmatic acknowledgement that uniform implementation of a complex language policy across India's diverse linguistic landscape requires more groundwork. The debate over the three-language formula is expected to continue as the government proceeds with NEP rollout across state and central boards.
key_facts: CBSE relaxes three-language policy for Classes 7, 8, and 9
Current Class 10 batch exempted from the policy
Two foreign language options now permitted
Karnataka state school body had opposed Kannada being treated as third language

stories.headline: With US AI off the table, India turns to Chinese models as alternatives to OpenAI and Anthropic
stories.what: India is increasingly looking at Chinese AI models as substitutes for leading US platforms like OpenAI and Anthropic, according to a report in The Economic Times. The shift is being driven by uncertain access to frontier American AI systems, pushing Indian enterprises and developers to explore alternatives from China. This represents a significant geopolitical tilt in India's AI adoption strategy at a time when the country is rapidly scaling its digital infrastructure.
stories.lens: For Indian businesses and startups building AI products, this shift could reshape which technology they depend on — and which foreign government's data policies they're subject to.
stories.article:
India is pivoting toward Chinese AI models as access to US frontier platforms such as OpenAI and Anthropic remains uncertain, a development that carries major strategic and commercial implications for the country's fast-growing technology sector. The Economic Times reports that Indian enterprises are actively evaluating Chinese AI alternatives to fill the gap left by the unpredictable availability of American models.

The uncertainty around US AI access stems from a combination of export control pressures, licensing concerns, and the broader geopolitical tension between Washington and the rest of the world over advanced technology. For Indian developers, startups, and large enterprises that have built workflows around platforms like ChatGPT or Claude, the inability to rely on those tools is a practical operational problem that demands immediate solutions.

The irony is not lost on analysts: India, which has maintained a careful distance from Chinese technology in sectors like telecom and consumer apps, may now be drawn toward Beijing-backed AI systems by the very restrictions its close partner the United States is imposing. Chinese models — several of which have demonstrated competitive performance benchmarks — are filling a vacuum that US policy is inadvertently creating.

The development also puts Nasscom and Indian policymakers in a difficult position, as they simultaneously advocate for domestic AI capability-building while managing short-term dependency on foreign systems. How India navigates this choice — between American platforms with uncertain access and Chinese alternatives with different risks — will define much of its AI strategy in the near term.
key_facts: India exploring Chinese AI models as alternatives to OpenAI and Anthropic
Shift driven by uncertain access to US frontier AI platforms
Reported by The Economic Times

stories.headline: 'Outright stupid': Ex-Meta exec warns US and European firms are ditching OpenAI and Anthropic for Chinese AI
stories.what: A former Meta executive has made a blunt prediction that enterprises in the United States and Europe will abandon OpenAI and Anthropic in favour of Chinese AI models, calling any resistance to that shift 'outright stupid'. The remarks, reported by NDTV Profit, add a provocative insider perspective to the rapidly evolving global AI competition. The ex-exec's view suggests that Chinese models have reached a competitive threshold that makes them commercially rational choices for cost-conscious enterprises.
stories.lens: When a former insider at one of the world's biggest AI companies says Western firms will switch to Chinese AI, it's a warning shot about how quickly the AI market's loyalties — and risks — can shift.
stories.article:
A former Meta executive has fired a sharp salvo into the global AI debate, declaring that enterprises across the United States and Europe will increasingly ditch OpenAI and Anthropic for Chinese AI alternatives — and that resisting such a move would be 'outright stupid'. The remarks, reported by NDTV Profit, represent one of the starkest public endorsements yet of Chinese AI competitiveness from someone with direct knowledge of the frontier model landscape.

The ex-exec's argument appears to rest on a straightforward commercial calculus: Chinese AI models have closed much of the capability gap with their American counterparts, while often being available at significantly lower cost or with fewer access restrictions. For large enterprises managing sprawling AI deployments, those differences can translate into meaningful savings and operational flexibility.

The comments land at a delicate moment. Governments in both the US and Europe are actively trying to limit Chinese AI adoption on national security grounds, warning that models developed under Chinese jurisdiction could expose sensitive enterprise data to state surveillance. The former executive's dismissal of such concerns as 'outright stupid' is likely to provoke pushback from policymakers who see the issue as far more than a cost-benefit calculation.

For India, already reported to be exploring Chinese AI models as OpenAI and Anthropic access remains uncertain, the ex-exec's remarks add an unexpected dimension: the shift away from US platforms is not just an emerging-market pragmatism story — it may be a global enterprise trend that even mature Western markets are beginning to follow.
key_facts: Ex-Meta executive predicts US and EU firms will abandon OpenAI and Anthropic
Called resistance to Chinese AI 'outright stupid'
Reflects growing competitive parity of Chinese AI models

stories.headline: Palantir and NVIDIA bring Nemotron AI models to sovereign AI deployments
stories.what: Palantir and NVIDIA have announced a collaboration to integrate NVIDIA's Nemotron AI models into Palantir's sovereign AI deployment infrastructure, according to Analytics India Magazine. The partnership targets governments and enterprises that require AI systems deployed within their own jurisdictions — without data leaving national or organisational boundaries. Nemotron, NVIDIA's family of enterprise-grade language models, is well-suited to high-security, compliance-sensitive environments where sovereign AI is a prerequisite.
stories.lens: As India pursues its own sovereign AI ambitions, the Palantir-NVIDIA model for secure, in-jurisdiction AI deployment offers a template — and a product — that Indian government and defence clients could look to adopt.
stories.article:
Palantir and NVIDIA have joined forces to bring NVIDIA's Nemotron AI models to sovereign AI deployments through Palantir's platform, in a partnership reported by Analytics India Magazine. The collaboration is aimed squarely at governments and large enterprises that cannot or will not send sensitive data to external cloud servers — a constituency that is growing rapidly as nations assert greater control over their AI infrastructure.

NVIDIA's Nemotron models are designed for enterprise and institutional use, offering strong performance on tasks like reasoning, summarisation, and code generation while being deployable on on-premise or sovereign cloud infrastructure. Palantir, known for its deep relationships with defence and intelligence agencies, provides the integration layer that makes such AI deployable within classified or highly regulated environments.

The partnership reflects a broader trend: as AI becomes central to government operations, national security, and critical infrastructure, the 'where does the data go?' question is becoming as important as 'how good is the model?'. Sovereign AI — AI that operates entirely within a nation's legal and physical jurisdiction — is emerging as a distinct product category, not just a policy aspiration.

For India, which has announced ambitions to develop and deploy AI for government services and defence applications, the Palantir-NVIDIA model is instructive. It demonstrates that cutting-edge AI capability and data sovereignty are not mutually exclusive — and that international technology partners are increasingly building products designed to meet that exact requirement.
key_facts: Palantir and NVIDIA partnering on sovereign AI deployments
NVIDIA's Nemotron models to be integrated into Palantir's platform
Targets governments and enterprises requiring in-jurisdiction AI

stories.headline: Nasscom urges India to test critical systems with OpenAI models amid frontier AI access uncertainty
stories.what: India's IT industry body Nasscom has advocated for testing the country's critical systems using OpenAI models, even as access to frontier AI platforms remains uncertain. The recommendation highlights the tension between India's ambition to build sovereign AI capabilities and its current dependence on foreign technology for high-stakes applications. Nasscom's intervention comes as Indian enterprises are caught between unreliable access to top US models and the risks of turning to alternatives.
stories.lens: Nasscom's push signals that India's most powerful tech lobby believes the country cannot yet go it alone on AI for critical infrastructure — a frank admission with real policy consequences.
stories.article:
Nasscom has called for India to use OpenAI models to stress-test and evaluate its critical systems, even as the availability of frontier AI platforms for Indian users and enterprises remains in flux. The industry lobby's position, reported by NDTV Profit, reflects the pragmatic reality facing Indian technology decision-makers: domestic AI models are not yet mature enough for high-stakes deployments, and waiting for certainty could delay crucial digital infrastructure work.

The recommendation is significant because it comes at a moment when India is simultaneously trying to reduce dependence on foreign technology — particularly American and Chinese platforms — while acknowledging that no credible domestic substitute yet exists for the most capable AI systems. Nasscom's stance amounts to a call for strategic engagement with OpenAI rather than a retreat from it.

The uncertainty around frontier model access is partly structural. Export controls, licensing conditions, and the pace of US regulatory changes around AI have made it difficult for Indian enterprises to plan long-term deployments around American platforms. Nasscom appears to be arguing that India should use the window of available access productively, running evaluations and building institutional knowledge, rather than deferring all AI integration until the geopolitical picture clears.

The broader implication is a policy dilemma: India must balance its digital sovereignty ambitions with the practical need to keep pace with global AI adoption. Nasscom's public advocacy for OpenAI usage in critical system testing is likely to intensify debate within government and industry about which foreign AI partners India can afford to trust — and under what conditions.
key_facts: Nasscom advocates testing India's critical systems with OpenAI models
Access to frontier AI models remains uncertain for India
Highlights tension between AI sovereignty goals and foreign dependency

stories.headline: Google takes a jab at Meta as delays mount on Meta's new AI model release to developers
stories.what: Google has sent a pointed message to Meta as the social media giant continues to delay the release of its newest AI model to developers, according to The Times of India. While specific details of Google's communication were not fully disclosed in the headline, the move is seen as an opportunistic competitive signal — Google positioning itself as the more developer-reliable AI platform. Meta's repeated postponements have frustrated the developer community that had been counting on early access.
stories.lens: For Indian developers and startups building on top of AI APIs, the reliability of platform release timelines directly affects product roadmaps — and Google is making the case that it is the safer bet.
stories.article:
Google has seized on Meta's repeated delays in releasing its latest AI model to the developer community, sending what The Times of India describes as a pointed message to its rival. While the precise content of Google's communication was not fully detailed in available reporting, the timing makes the competitive intent clear: as Meta stumbles on its developer release schedule, Google is positioning its own AI offerings as the dependable alternative.

Meta has been expected to release a new frontier AI model to external developers, but the timeline has slipped multiple times. Such delays carry real costs beyond reputation — developers who plan integrations, build applications, and pitch products to clients depend on predictable access windows. When a major platform misses its own release dates repeatedly, developers begin hedging with other providers.

Google, which has been aggressively expanding its Gemini model family and developer tooling under Google Cloud, is well-placed to absorb any developers who grow impatient with Meta's pace. The implicit message in Google's public positioning is that its developer ecosystem is open, on schedule, and ready to onboard.

For the Indian developer community — one of the world's largest and most active consumers of AI APIs — the Google-Meta dynamic is more than a Silicon Valley rivalry. It shapes which platforms Indian startups build on, which ecosystems they invest time learning, and ultimately which foreign technology companies gain structural influence over India's AI economy.
key_facts: Meta has repeatedly delayed release of its new AI model to developers
Google publicly signalled its position as a reliable developer alternative
Competition for developer mindshare intensifying between Google and Meta

stories.headline: FIFA World Cup 2026: latest scores and full results
stories.what: The latest FIFA World Cup 2026 results in full — scorelines, goalscorers and what's coming up next, all in IST.
stories.lens: Every matchday, tracked in your time zone.
stories.article:
The FIFA World Cup 2026 group stage continued with another full matchday. Here are the latest results in full:

South Africa 0-1 Canada. Scorers: Stephan Eustáquio 90+2'. Half-time: 0-0. Venue: Los Angeles (Inglewood).

Algeria 3-3 Austria (Group J). Scorers: Rafik Belghali 45', Riyad Mahrez 60', Riyad Mahrez 90+3', Marko Arnautovic 28', Marcel Sabitzer 55', Sasa Kalajdzic 90+6'. Half-time: 1-1. Venue: Kansas City.
